coredata.dc:title: Dual signal amplification coupling dual inhibition effect for fabricating photoelectrochemical chlorpyrifos biosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Hanping
Yan, Yuting
Hao, Nan
Liu, Qian
Qian, Jing
Chen, Saibo
Wang, Kun
coredata.dc:description: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) detection is an attractive analytical tool as it allows for an elegant and sensitive assay. However, designing a novel detection strategy to achieve an excellent PEC analytical performance is still highly challenging. Herein we design a novel photoelectrochemical (PEC) chlorpyrifos biosensor based on dual signal amplification strategy coupling dual inhibition effect. Dual signal amplification strategy was achieved by coupling the graphene quantum dots sensitized CuFe2O4 magnetic nanocrystal clusters (GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs) with the amplification of enzymolysis products. In this biosensing architecture, the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs prepared by electrostatic adsorption showed nearly 4-fold and 30-fold enhancement photocurrent compared with the pure CuFe2O4 MNCs and GQDs, respectively. And the contact angle measurement demonstrated that the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs exhibited good biocompatibility. Based on all these above advantages, the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs were immobilized on the magnetic electrode surface by a fast and simple magnetism-assisted assembly, and the acetylcholinesterase (AChE) was further coated on the surface of the as-prepared multifunctional electrode. Due to thiocholine (enzymolysis products) acts as a sacrificial electron donor to scavenge the holes, compared with the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs modified electrode in the acetylthiocholine chloride solution, the photocurrent of the resulting AChE-based biosensor was further significantly enhanced. Based on the dual inhibition of AChE activity by chlorpyrifos and the formation of Cu-chlorpyrifos complex hindered the electron transfer of CuFe2O4 MNCs toward the electrode surface, the proposed AChE-based biosensor can be applied to the quantification of chlorpyrifos with a linear range from 0.001μgmL−1 to 1μgmL−1 and a detection limit of 0.3ngmL−1 (S/N=3). This novel dual signal amplification strategy opens up a new avenue for achieving high sensitivity in the field of PEC biosensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chlorpyrifos
CuFe2O4
Graphene quantum dot
Dual signal amplification
Dual inhibition
Photoelectrochemical biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PEC behaviors and EIS of the GQDs−CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE
Construction of the PEC sensor for chlorpyrifos detection
Optimization for the PEC responses at the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE
The fabrication of PEC biosensor for chlorpyrifos detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration for the enhanced PEC signal of GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs and the PEC chlorpyrifos sensor was fabricated and its detecting mechanism.

(A) TEM image and (B) TEM image at higher magnification of GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs; (C) XRD patterns and (D) magnetization curves of CuFe2O4 MNCs (a) and GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs (b) at 300K, inset: photographic images of GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs suspension in the absence (left) and presence (right) of an external magnetic field.
XPS curves of as-prepared GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs: (A) Survey spectrum, (B) Cu 2p, (C) Fe 2p and (D) C1s.

Photocurrent responses (A) of CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE (a), GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE (b) and GQDs/mGCE (c); (B) EIS of bare mGCE (a), GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE (b), CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE (c) in 0.1M KCl containing 5mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−; (C) The photocurrent responses of the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) in the presence of (a) 0, (b) 0.02, (c) 0.04, (d) 0.08, (e) 0.2, (f) 0.5, (g) 0.8, (h) 1.0, (i) 2.0, and (j) 5.0μgmL−1, inset: The calibration curve for chlorpyrifos determination. Error bars: +S.D., n=5; (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of 0.1mgmL−1 CuFe2O4 MNCs (a), (b) 0.1mgmL−1 chlorpyrifos and (c) 0.1mgmL−1 CuFe2O4 MNCs containing 0.1mgmL−1, inset: EIS of GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE before (a) and after (b) the addition of 0.1mgmL−1 of chlorpyrifos.

Contact angle graphs of (A) CuFe2O4 MNCs and (B) GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs; (C) The photocurrent responses of GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE (a), AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) without (b) and with (c) 0.7mmolL−1 ATCl; (D) Time-based photocurrent response of AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE (a) in 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.7mmolL−1 ATCl under visible irradiation repeated every 50s.

(A) The photocurrent responses of AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE in 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.7mmolL−1 ATCl before (a) and after (b) incubation with 0.05μgmL−1 chlorpyrifos; (B) The photocurrent responses of the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.7mmolL−1 ATCl after incubation in (a) 0, (b) 0.001, (c) 0.005, (d) 0.02, (e) 0.05, (f) 0.1, (g) 0.5, (h) 1.0, (i) 2.0, (j) 5.0μgmL−1 chlorpyrifos solution for 5min, inset: The calibration curve for chlorpyrifos determination. Error bars: +S.D., n=5.

(A) The structural formulas of the tested pesticide compounds and (B) effects of different pesticides on the photocurrent intensity of the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.7mmolL−1 ATCl before and after incubation in same concentration of 0.1μgmL−1 chlorpyrifos, carbaryl, parathion-methyl, ethoprophos, acetamiprid and pentachlorophenol. (C) The interference of nitrobenzene, Ca2+, Mg2+, SO4 2− and NO2 − separately on the ΔI of the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.7mmolL−1 ATCl after incubated with 0.05μgmL−1 chlorpyrifos; (D) Stability of the PEC sensor over 20 days in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.7mmolL−1 ATCl after incubated with 0.05μgmL−1 chlorpyrifos.
Analysis of real samples with different concentrations of chlorpyrifos (n=3).

head.ce:title: Dual signal amplification coupling dual inhibition effect for fabricating photoelectrochemical chlorpyrifos biosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, Institute of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, Institute of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, Institute of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China.
Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, Institute of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) detection is an attractive analytical tool as it allows for an elegant and sensitive assay. However, designing a novel detection strategy to achieve an excellent PEC analytical performance is still highly challenging. Herein we design a novel photoelectrochemical (PEC) chlorpyrifos biosensor based on dual signal amplification strategy coupling dual inhibition effect. Dual signal amplification strategy was achieved by coupling the graphene quantum dots sensitized CuFe2O4 magnetic nanocrystal clusters (GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs) with the amplification of enzymolysis products. In this biosensing architecture, the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs prepared by electrostatic adsorption showed nearly 4-fold and 30-fold enhancement photocurrent compared with the pure CuFe2O4 MNCs and GQDs, respectively. And the contact angle measurement demonstrated that the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs exhibited good biocompatibility. Based on all these above advantages, the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs were immobilized on the magnetic electrode surface by a fast and simple magnetism-assisted assembly, and the acetylcholinesterase (AChE) was further coated on the surface of the as-prepared multifunctional electrode. Due to thiocholine (enzymolysis products) acts as a sacrificial electron donor to scavenge the holes, compared with the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs modified electrode in the acetylthiocholine chloride solution, the photocurrent of the resulting AChE-based biosensor was further significantly enhanced. Based on the dual inhibition of AChE activity by chlorpyrifos and the formation of Cu-chlorpyrifos complex hindered the electron transfer of CuFe2O4 MNCs toward the electrode surface, the proposed AChE-based biosensor can be applied to the quantification of chlorpyrifos with a linear range from 0.001μgmL−1 to 1μgmL−1 and a detection limit of 0.3ngmL−1 (S/N=3). This novel dual signal amplification strategy opens up a new avenue for achieving high sensitivity in the field of PEC biosensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensors have aroused extensive interests in the fields of biology, medicine and environment monitoring [1–3], due to their advantages of simple operation, excellent accuracy, and low-cost [4–8]. Nowadays, to achieve an excellent PEC analytical performance, one major challenge is to amplify the detectable signal during the measurement. To realize this issue, various signal amplification strategies have been exploited. The strategies can be divided into two categories depending on the components of a PEC sensing system, a proper PEC transducer and an electron/hole donor. One of the signal amplification strategies is exploiting innovative PEC transducer and designing appropriate sensitized structures. Another is to get a more efficient electron donor which is produced by biocatalysis reaction chain. In a first aspect, the various PEC transducers, especially semiconductors-based transducers (such as TiO2 [9–11], CdS [10,12,13], CdSe [14,15], BiOX [16,17], gold nanoclusters [18] and so on) have been investigated widely in PEC sensors due to their excellent PEC properties. Thus, searching for innovative nanomaterials with excellent PEC properties is obviously desirable to improve the performance of the PEC biosensors.

CuFe2O4 nanomaterial, as multifunctional magnetic material, has been widely applied in lithium-ion storage and photocatalysis degradation due to its electrical properties, low cost, high abundance and environmental friendliness [19,20]. Particularly, CuFe2O4 possesses unique optical and narrow band gap (1.4eV) [21,22], which is a promising candidate of the visible-light-responsive photocatalysts. However, the inherent poor conductivity [23,24] and the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs [25] limited its potential application in the electrochemical field, especially PEC field. As an alternative strategy, appropriate doping with carbonaceous materials can improve the performances of CuFe2O4 nanomaterials, effectively. According to the previous reports, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) could be used as an enhanced material for improving the performance of pure nanomaterials by GQDs sensitization [26,27]. Thus, as a signal amplification strategy, the photoelectrical performance of CuFe2O4 could be improved by GQDs sensitization.

Besides exploiting new photoactive materials and designing appropriate sensitized structures, selecting an efficient electron donor is also crucial to signal amplification. Up to now, to amplify the signals, almost all recent works have resorted to the use of various enzymes as biocatalysts to produce the electron donor. Such as ascorbic acid (AA) is in situ generated by alkaline phosphatase hydrolyzing the substrate 2-phospho-l-ascorbic acid [10,12,28], and thiocholine is produced from the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine catalyzed by acetylcholinesterase (AChE). Among kinds of electron donor species, AA and thiocholine have demonstrated themselves as an ideal candidate for signal amplification in PEC detection.

Chlorpyrifos, one of the most frequently used organophosphate pesticides, controls a broad spectrum of insects of economically important crops [29–31]. However, the use of this compound could elicit the inhibition of AChE and lead to the disruption of nerve function [31,32]. Thus, it is of extremely significance for the detection of chlorpyrifos in environmental samples and agricultural products. Herein, we developed a novel PEC biosensor based on dual signal amplification strategy by coupling the GQDs sensitized CuFe2O4 magnetic nanocrystal clusters (CuFe2O4 MNCs) with the amplification of enzymolysis products for the detection of chlorpyrifos. GQDs can effectively improve the PEC property of CuFe2O4 MNCs, and the PEC signal was further amplified by the enzymolysis products. The inhibition of AChE activity by chlorpyrifos prevented the production of thiocholine, and the formation of Cu-chlorpyrifos complex hindered the electron transfer of CuFe2O4 MNCs towards the electrode surface. The inhibitions above resulted in the obvious decrease of photocurrents. Based on the dual inhibitions, a proposed AChE-based biosensor can be applied to the detection of chlorpyrifos. This PEC biosensor showed good performance in chlorpyrifos monitoring with a rapid response and low detection limit.

Acetylcholinesterase (AChE, Type C3389, 500Umg−1 from electric eel), acetylthiocholine chloride (ATCl) and carbaryl were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (USA). Chlorpyrifos, carbaryl, parathion-methy and acetamiprid were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Pentachlorophenol (PCP) and ethoprophos were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. Pyrolyzing citric acid (CA), CuCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, NaAc, polyethylene glycol 20 000 and ethylene glycol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. PBS (0.1M, pH 7.0) was used as the supporting electrolyte, which was prepared by mixing stock standard solutions of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4, and adjusted the pH with 0.1M H3PO4 or NaOH solution. Other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification, and all solutions were prepared with twice-distilled water.

Zeta potentials were measured on a Malvern ZEN2600 Zetasizer Nano Z. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was taken with a JEOL 2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan) operated at 200kV. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on a VG MultiLab 2000 system with a monochromatic Mg-Kα source operated at 20kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted on a Bruker D8 diffractometer with high-intensity Cu Kα (λ=1.54Å). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by UV–2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The magnetization curves were obtained at room temperature on an LDJ 9600 (LDJ Electronics, Troy, MI) vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), and the static water contact angles were measured with a commercial instrument (CAM 200, KSV Instruments Ltd., Helsinki, Finland).

All the electrochemical and PEC measurements were conducted using CHI660B electrochemical analyzer (Chen Hua Instruments, Shanghai, China) and recorded by a conventional three-electrode system, where a magnetic glassy carbon electrode (mGCE, 10mm in diameter and 65mm in length) with an inserted glassy carbon (3mm in diameter and 2mm in depth) worked as working electrode. The mGCE was purchased from Tianjin Incole Union Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin China), Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl solution) as reference electrode and platinum wire as counter electrode, respectively. The PEC measurement was performed in 0.1M PBS at 0V, and a 250W Xe lamp (Beijing Trusttech Co. Ltd.) was used as the visible light source with an intensity (passing through a 400nm UV-cut filter) of 100mWcm−2. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in a 0.1M KCl solution containing 5mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− with a frequency range from 0.01Hz to 10kHz at 0.23V, and the amplitude of the applied sine wave potential in each case was 5mV.

The GQDs were prepared by directly CA according to the previous literature [33]. The CuFe2O4 MNCs were prepared using a hydrothermal method with some modifications [34]. Briefly, CuCl2 (0.34g, 2.5mmol) and FeCl3·6H2O (1.35g, 5mmol) were dissolved in ethylene glycol (40mL) to form a clear solution, followed by the addition of NaAc (3.6g) and polyethylene glycol 20 000 (1.0g). The mixture was stirred vigorously for 30min and then sealed in a teflonlined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated to and maintained at 200°C for 8h and allowed to cool to room temperature. The black products were washed several times with ethanol and dried at 60°C for 6h.

The GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs were achieved as follows: 100mg CuFe2O4 was dispersed into 50mL above GQDs water solution (2mg/mL). The final mixture was stirred for 30min. At last, the precipitate was under magnetic separation and dried in vacuum at 45°C for 24h to obtain the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs.

Prior to each modification, mGCE was first polished with polishing clothes and 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05μm alumina slurry. After successive sonication in ethanol and water, the electrode was rinsed with water and allowed to dry at room temperature. 8mg of the as-prepared GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs was dispersed in 200mL water. After that, the pretreated mGCE was immersed shallowly in 3mL of the above suspension nearly all the hybrid was assembled on the electrode surface in 5min by magnetism without any fixing agent (labeled as GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE). For comparison, pristine CuFe2O4 MNCs modified mGCE was also prepared in the same way (labeled as CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE). The GQDs of the same concentration were dropped on the surface of mGCE (labeled as GQDs/mGCE). The CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE was finally coated with 6μL AChE solution (293UmL−1) and incubated at 25°C for 30min. After evaporation of water, the modified electrode was washed with 0.1M PBS to remove the unbound AChE, and the resulted AChE–GQDs−CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE/GCE was stored at 4°C when not in use.

The characterization of the as-prepared GQDs was displayed in Fig. S1. As shown in Fig. S1A, the resulting GQDs were uniformly distributed with about 1–5nm in size. The inset was the related TEM image in higher magnification. The distribution of the diameters size of GQDs was evaluated statistically through measuring the diameter of 100 GQDs in the selected TEM images. It was noted that the diameters size of GQDs was distributed mainly between 1.0 and 5.0nm with an average diameter of about 3nm (Fig. S1B). Fig. S1C showed the UV–vis absorption of GQDs, a typical absorption peak at ca. 365nm can be observed. And inset of Fig. S1C showed the optical images of the GQDs with blue emission under UV illumination. The emission spectra of as-prepared GQDs with the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength at 362 and 460nm, respectively, as showed in Fig. S1D.

The GQDs onto the surface of the CuFe2O4 MNCs was achieved through the electrostatic interaction, which was proved by zeta potential analyzer. The surfaces of the CuFe2O4 MNCs were positive charges with a zeta potential of +18.3mV. The zeta potential of GQDs was measured to be −31.4mV and thus possessing negatively charged surfaces were subsequently attached to the surface of the CuFe2O4 MNCs via electrostatic self-assembly. The zeta potential of GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs was −5.9mV, which demonstrated that the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs have been successfully synthesized by electrostatic self-assembly.

The morphology and structure of the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs were investigated using TEM. From the TEM image (Fig. 1 A), it can be clearly observed that the synthesized GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs exhibit nearly spherical in shape [35] and had an average diameter of 100nm. The secondary structure of the CuFe2O4 MNCs can be observed more clearly in the TEM image with higher magnification, as shown in Fig. 1B, which was assembled with primary small particles with a size of about 6–15nm [36]. It was found that the surface of the CuFe2O4 MNCs was very rough, which was maybe contributed to the coat of small-sized GQDs on the surface of CuFe2O4 MNCs.

The crystal structures of the as-prepared CuFe2O4 MNCs and GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs were examined using XRD diffraction patterns. As can be seen from Fig. 1C, the position and relative intensity of diffraction peaks are in accordance with that of CuFe2O4 (JCPDS, card NO.: 25–0283), and the diffraction peaks at 2θ=18.5, 30.1, 35.6, 38.7, 42.9, 53.1, 57.6, and 62.8° are ascribed to the (111), (220), (311), (320), (400), (422), (511), and (440), which suggest that the cubic spinel structure CuFe2O4 was exclusively formed (curve a) [37]. It was found that the peak at (311) was the highest intensity implying the oriented growth of the sample alongside the (311) direction. And the relatively high peak intensities imply that the products are highly crystalline. In addition, GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs (curve b) showed similar XRD pattern with pure CuFe2O4 MNCs, suggesting that the presence of GQDs in the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs did not affect the crystalline structure of CuFe2O4 MNCs. a broader (002) peak of GQDs centered at around 25.7° was observed, which was in agreement with the previous report [33].

The magnetic properties of CuFe2O4 MNCs and GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs were studied using a VSM at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 1D. The saturation magnetization (MS) value of CuFe2O4 MNCs (curve a) and GQDs−CuFe2O4 MNCs (curve b) was 32.06 and 16.02emug−1, respectively. Obviously, after combining with GQDs, its magnetic response was affected due to mixing with non-magnetic material [38]. As demonstrated in the inset of Fig. 1D, GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs could be directed to specific locations when manipulated by an external magnetic field, which was in favor of the magnetic field-driven separation and the magneto-controlled modification onto electrode surface under an external.

To further investigate the elemental composition and chemical nature of GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs, XPS measurements have been performed. In the survey spectrum (Fig. 2 A), the elements Cu, Fe, C and O can be observed clearly, which show the existence of GQDs in the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs. From the Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 2B), it was found that the peak at 933.5eV is from Cu 2p3/2, with a satellite peak at 943.4eV, which provides clear evidence for the presence of Cu2+ [39–41]. The Fe 2p spectra was showed in Fig. 2C, the two strong peaks that appeared at 711.3 and 724.8eV, which were assigned to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively. Two accompanying satellite peaks visible at binding energies of around 719.0eV was indicative of the presence of Fe3+ cations [42]. The high resolution C1s XPS spectra were shown in Fig. 2D, the binding energy of 284.7, 286.2 and 287.5eV are attributed to the CC and CC bonds, COH and CO oxygen-containing carbon bands, respectively [43], indicating the as-prepared GQDs were functionalized with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC behaviors and EIS of the GQDs−CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE
ce:section.ce:para: The PEC behaviors of electrodes with different modifications were investigated in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) at the applied potential of 0V under visible irradiation. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the photocurrent of 0.12μA was observed for pure CuFe2O4 MNCs modified mGCE (curve a). And a small photocurrent was observed at GQDs/mGCE (curve c). It is clear that the photocurrent of the GQDs−CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE (curve b) was nearly 4-fold and 30-fold enhancement than that of CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE (curve a) and GQDs/mGCE (curve c), respectively. Moreover, the photocurrent of GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE exhibited more stable than that of CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE, which was attributed to the introduction of GQDs in the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs. Good electrical conductivity of GQDs could accelerate the electron transfer [44,45]. The mechanism explanation of the proposed transducer as follows: Upon irradiation with visible light, CuFe2O4 MNCs were excited and the electrons transformed from valance band to conduction band, and generated electron-hole pairs, in the presence of GQDs, the electrons transferred from CuFe2O4 MNCs to GQDs, and at last to the mGCE to generate photocurrent. Due to the excellent electrical conductivity of GQDs in the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs, the GQDs could accelerate the electron transfer, consequently reducing the recombination rate of the photogenerated electrons and holes. Therefore, GQDs played a significant role in enhancing and stabilizing the PEC activity of CuFe2O4 MNCs (Amplification I).

To further study the interface properties of different electrodes, the EIS was investigated by using [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as the redox probe. As shown in Fig. 3B, it can be found that the electron-transfer resistance (R ct) value of bare mGCE (curve a), GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE (curve b) and CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE (curve c) were about 70, 108, and 180Ω, respectively. The result indicated that the couple of GQDs with CuFe2O4 MNCs was beneficial for enhancing electron transport ability of pristine CuFe2O4 MNCs [46]. Besides, a smaller Rct of the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs indicated that more effective separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and faster interfacial charge transfer had occurred [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the PEC sensor for chlorpyrifos detection
ce:section.ce:para: The photocurrent of GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE decreased with the addition of chlorpyrifos, which showed the chlorpyrifos had an inhibition effect on the PEC response of GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE. The photocurrent response of GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE in the presence of different concentrations of chlorpyrifos at an applied potential of 0V with light illumination has been investigated, as shown in Fig. 3C. A linear relationship was obtained between the photocurrent intensity (I) and the logarithm of the chlorpyrifos concentration (inset of Fig. 3C). The linear equation was I=0.1173lg(c/μgmL−1)−0.2234 (R2 =0.996) over a concentration range from 0.02 to 1μgmL−1. The detection limit for chlorpyrifos was calculated to be 6.6ngmL−1 (S/N=3). It may be interpreted as follows: the chlorpyrifos moiety with CN and PS bonds could coordinate with the metal ions [47,48]. The strong coordinative interaction would drive chlorpyrifos molecules to assemble onto the surface of the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs, resulting in the decrease of the photocurrent response (Inhibition I). The intermolecular interaction between CuFe2O4 MNCs and chlorpyrifos was confirmed by detailed UV–vis absorbance spectra and EIS (Fig. 3D). A strong absorption of CuFe2O4 MNCs was observed at 403.8nm (curve a), and the absorption of chlorpyrifos was observed at 289.8nm (curve b). In the presence of 0.1mgmL−1 the maximum absorption wavelength of CuFe2O4 MNCs was slightly red-shifted and its absorbance intensity decreased dramatically (curve c) and the absorption spectrum of CuFe2O4 MNCs-chlorpyrifos system displayed a new peak at 256.5nm (curve c), which was a kind of obvious evidence that they have formed the Cu-chlorpyrifos complex with new structure [47,48]. The strong coordinative interaction would drive chlorpyrifos molecules to assemble onto the surface of the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs as confirmed by the Ret value increased from 93.5 to 739.3Ω before (inset a of Fig. 3D) and after (inset b of Fig. 3D) adding the chlorpyrifos. Based on this, a PEC sensor was fabricated for detection of chlorpyrifos. However, the sensitivity of this work wasn’t satisfying. According to previous reports [49,50], upon visible light irradiation, the photocurrent of AChE–based electrode would be significantly influenced by the addition of organophosphate pesticides (OPs). Based on the inhibition effect of OPs on the photocurrent of AChE–based electrode, the PEC chlorpyrifos biosensor was further designed.

The biocompatibility of an interface can be characterized by its hydrophilicity, which can be qualitative by measuring the contact angle of the substrate [51]. Thus, the contact angles of CuFe2O4 MNCs and GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs were measured. The contact angle of the CuFe2O4 MNCs was 82.3° (Fig. 4 A). When the GQDs were doped into CuFe2O4 MNCs, the contact angle decreased drastically due to the presence of plenty of oxygen functional groups such as COOH (Fig. 4B). Thus, the GQDs in GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs not only improved the electro transfer, but also increased the surface hydrophilicity. And this kind of interface was suitable for facile immobilization of biomolecules [52,53].

The photocurrent responses of the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE and AChE–GQDs−CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE electrodes were shown in Fig. 4C. Compared with the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE, the photocurrent response of the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE slightly enhanced with the introduction of AChE (curve b), which was probably due to the light-harvesting effect of AChE toward visible light as well as the permission of electron or energy migration between AChE and GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs [49,50]. Interestingly, a significantly enhanced photocurrent in the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE (curve c) was observed with the further addition of ATCl (an enzyme substrate) to the electrolyte, which could be explained as follows: After the immobilization of AChE, when this system is exposed to a solution containing ATCl, the immobilized AChE can hydrolyze ATCl into acetate and thiocholine [49]. Upon irradiation, thiocholine acts as a sacrificial electron donor to scavenge the holes and improves the efficiency of charge separation, leading to a prominent increase in the photocurrent (Amplification II) [50,54].

Further, the PEC stability of the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE was also investigated in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) in the presence of 0.7mmolL−1ATCl. As shown in Fig. 4D, the PEC response of the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE was repeated 10 times every 50s under visible irradiation, and the photocurrent response was ca. 0.83μA and did not show any obvious change, which indicated that the photocurrent intensity of the modified electrode was very stable and beneficial for the construction of PEC biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for the PEC responses at the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve high-performance visible light-activated PEC responses of AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE for chlorpyrifos determination, we further optimized the experimental parameters, such as effects of solution pH, ATCl concentration and inhibition time on the PEC behaviors of the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE.

The concentration of ATCl was one of the most influential parameters. When the concentration of ATCl is too low, the photocurrent generated by the enzyme electrode is too small that the determination of chlorpyrifos is hardly feasible. However, too high concentration would cause all the active centers of AChE being occupied by the substrate and insensible to the inhibitor [49]. Thus, the effect of ATCl concentration on the photocurrent responses of the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs film was investigated in this work (Fig. S2A). The photocurrent increased gradually with the increase of the ATCl concentration, and when the concentration of ATCl reached 0.7mmolL−1, the photocurrent increased slowly and tended to a stable signal. Thus, the concentration of 0.7mmolL−1 ATCl is an optimized value in this experiment.

The effect of pH on the PEC behavior of AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE was performed in 0.1M PBS containing 0.7mmolL−1 ATCl with different pH values. As shown in Fig. S2B, the PEC intensity increased with the increasing of pH from 5.0 to 7.0, and then dropped at high pH from 7.0 to 9.0, indicating that the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE exhibited a relatively stable PEC signal in a wide pH range. Thus, pH 7.0 was chosen as the optimal pH value in the following experiments to keep the bioactivity of AChE on the GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs film.

Inhibition time is an important influential parameter in pesticide analysis. So the dependence of inhibition time on the chlorpyrifos inhibition was also investigated, as shown in Fig. S2C, chlorpyrifos displayed an increasing inhibition on AChE with the immersion time increased. When the immersion time was longer than 5min, the inhibition tended to a stable value, which indicated that the binding interaction with active target groups in the enzyme reached saturation. Therefore, 5min inhibition time was used in our experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fabrication of PEC biosensor for chlorpyrifos detection
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the results above, we explored the quantitative range of PEC detection for chlorpyrifos. Fig. 5B presented the effect of different concentrations of chlorpyrifos on the photocurrent response of AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE. It is clearly that the photocurrents decreased with the increase of chlorpyrifos concentration, and the standard calibration curve for chlorpyrifos detection was illustrated in the inset of Fig. 5B. The photocurrents displayed a linear range of 1ngmL−1–1μgmL−1, and the linear equation was I=0.1905lg(c/μgmL−1)−0.1757 (R2 =0.991). The detection limit was 0.3ngmL−1 (S/N=3). The AChE−based electrode possessed a wider linear range and lower detection limit than that of GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE, which might be attributed to the dual inhibition effect on AChE and GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs by chlorpyrifos. The proposed biosensor for chlorpyrifos is tested against other pesticides, e.g., parathion-methyl, pentachlorophenol, carbaryl, ethoprophos and acetamiprid as shown in Fig. 6 B. The other pesticides had smaller influence on the PEC response than that of chlorpyrifos. The chlorpyrifos not only as a OPs, but also with both CN and PS bond, which were able to turn off the PEC response of the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE and resulted in a remarkable photocurrent decrease due to the dual inhibition effect on AChE and GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs by chlorpyrifos. The other OPs have some inhibition effect on AChE and turn off the PEC response of the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE. And the acetamiprid only with CN bond, it has little influence to the PEC response of the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE. In addition, the PEC signal for 0.7mmolL−1 ATCl was compared with the signal obtained in the presence of the other interference species, such as nitrobenzen, Mg2+, Ca2+, NO2 − and SO4 2− (5mM) were investigated (Fig. 6C). No obvious inhibition behavior can be observed with the peak currents of ATCl varied slightly.

The long-term stability of the PEC biosensor was investigated during 20 days, as shown in Fig. 6D, which was examined by measuring the photocurrent responses of AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.7mmolL−1 ATCl in 0.1M PBS after incubation with 0.1μgmL−1 chlorpyrifos and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 6.3% was obtained, indicating the reliability of the 20 days.

With the purpose of investigating the potential applicability of the proposed PEC method applied to the real samples, the PEC sensor has been used for the detection of the chlorpyrifos in river with a standard addition method. The extraction of river water was obtained by filtering with 0.22μm millipore filter and three chlorpyrifos standard solutions with different concentrations were added into the extraction solution, respectively. The recoveries of spiked chlorpyrifos ranged from 95.3% to 101.2% with satisfactory RSD for three independent measurements were obtained, indicating that the proposed PEC sensor could be used for the analysis of real samples (Table 1 ).

The extraction solutions of cucumbers were prepared as follows: 50μL of different concentrations of chlorpyrifos (0.2μgmL−1, 1μgmL−1 and 2μgmL−1) were transferred onto the peel, then allowed to dry for 12h at room conditions. Swab sticks were soaked in water–methanol (1:1) for 10s prior to using. The chlorpyrifos −exposed surface (1×1cm) was swabbed for 1min. Then, the swab was soaked and vortexed in 1mL of water–methanol for 3min. The final amount of chlorpyrifos exposed to the peel surface was calculated to be 0.01μgmL−1, 0.05μgmL−1 and 0.1μgmL−1, respectively. The recoveries were found to be in the range of 98.2%–100.9% with the RSD lower than 5.2%, as displayed in Table 1, indicating the biosensor had a great potential for practical applications.

By integrating GQDs sensitized CuFe2O4 MNCs and the amplification of enzymolysis products into one PEC system, a novel PEC biosensor based on dual signal amplification for the detection of chlorpyrifos were successfully fabricated. The advantages are as follows: (1) the GQDs as a sensitizer, not only enhance the photocurrent of pure CuFe2O4, but also improve the hydrophilicity of CuFe2O4 MNCs; (2) The PEC signal was further amplified by the enzymatic product; (3) Based on dual inhibition effects of chlorpyrifos on AChE and GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs, a chlorpyrifos biosensor was successfully constructed. (4) With the novel PEC strategy, this method achieved excellent performance on the merits of a wide linear range and low detection limit. Thus, this design principle with dual signal amplification can be extended to other biosensors and provide a highly sensitive platform for various molecules.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21175061 and 21375050), the National Key Technology Research and Development Program of the Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China (No. 2014BAD08B03), Innovation Project of Science and Technology for College Graduates of Jiangsu Province (No. KYLX15_1085), a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2014-37), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project (No.2015T80517), Qing Lan Project and Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology (No. NZ201109).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.07.072.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The characterizations of the GQDs were shown in Fig. S1 in supporting information. The figure captions of optimization for the PEC responses at the AChE–GQDs–CuFe2O4 MNCs/mGCE were shown in Fig. S2.
The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Fabrication of glutathione photoelectrochemical biosensor using graphene-CdS nanocomposites

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Prof. Hanping Mao, Professor of Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology (Ministry of Education of China) in Jiangsu University, and Member of Discipline Appraisal Group of State Council. His research interests mainly include: the intelligent agricultural equipment and technology, precision applying fertilizer and pesticide, detection technology of life information, and etc.

Yuting Yan obtained her M.Sc. from school of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University (China) in 2014. Now she is a PhD student at School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her current research interests focus on the preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in pesticides and biomolecules analysis in agriculture.

Nan Hao obtained his Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry from Nanjing University (China) in 2015. Now he is a lecturer at School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His current research interests focus on the preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in bioanalysis.

Qian Liu obtained her Ph.D. from Jiangsu University (China) in 2014. Now she is a postdoctor at School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her current research interests focus on the preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in pesticides and biomolecules analysis in agriculture.

Jing Qian obtained her Ph.D. in Material Physics and Chemistry from Southeast University (China) in 2012. Now she is an associate professor at School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her current research interests focus on the preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in bioanalysis.

Saibo Chen is currently studying for master degree in school of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her main research interest lies in the field of electrochemical biosensor.

Prof. Kun Wang, Professor at School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His Ph.D. was obtained from Nanjing University (China) in 2005 under the direction of Prof. Hongyuan Chen. His research interests concentrate in designing and preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in analytical chemistry, energy, and environment.
